nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater streams and rivers associated with ancient forest peat swamps; slow-moving or still water heavily shaded by forest canopy, with soft, acidic water stained brown by tannins from decomposing leaf litter and peat

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on zooplankton, small worms, and tiny crustaceans. In captivity, generally requires very small live or frozen foods such as baby brine shrimp nauplii, micro worms, daphnia, cyclops, and rotifers. May accept high-quality micro pellets or finely crushed flakes, though live and frozen foods are typically preferred and help maintain health and coloration. Food particle size is critical given their very small mouths.

summary: Among the smallest fish commonly available in the aquarium trade at 1.5–2.5 cm, the Dwarf Rasbora (Boraras maculatus) is a blackwater nano species from Southeast Asia. Best kept in groups of eight or more in soft, acidic conditions with dense planting.
careNotes: One of the smallest cyprinids available in the aquarium hobby, rarely exceeding 2.5 cm. Native to ancient peat swamp blackwater biotopes across Peninsular Malaysia, southern Thailand, Sumatra, and Singapore, where water is extremely soft and acidic. Water chemistry is considered critical for this species — soft, acidic conditions in the pH 4.0–6.5 range are generally advised, and adaptation to hard or alkaline water tends to be poor. A well-established, biologically mature tank is recommended before introduction, as juveniles can be sensitive to unstable water conditions. Dense planting (Java moss, Cryptocoryne, floating plants), leaf litter, and driftwood help recreate the blackwater biotope and provide the cover these naturally timid fish tend to need. Low to moderate lighting is preferred, and current should be gentle. Three small feedings per day are generally advised given the species' high metabolism and small stomach capacity. Their tiny size requires very small food particles — baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms are reliable staples. Though entirely peaceful, their diminutive size and timid nature make them unsuitable for general community tanks; they are best suited to species tanks or carefully curated nano setups with similarly small, peaceful companions. The family placement of Boraras has been revised in some recent scientific literature to Danionidae, though Cyprinidae remains the name most widely used in the aquarium hobby.
breedingNotes: Moderate difficulty. Continuous egg-scatterer with no parental care; adults will consume their own eggs and fry if not separated. Breeding typically occurs in a dedicated small tank (around 10–20 L) with spawning mops or fine-leaved plants such as Java moss to catch eggs, pH around 5.8–6.3, and very soft water (1–3 dGH). Spawning occurs daily in good conditions. Eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days; fry are very small and generally require infusoria or paramecium initially before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms after 7–10 days. Males tend to be slimmer and may show slightly brighter spot coloration; females appear noticeably rounder when gravid.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary due to scientific depth and husbandry detail. Aquarium Co-Op (slice primary) returned 404 for this species page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-check for taxonomy, native range, size, and water parameters. Lists family as Danionidae (reclassified) and max size 2.5 cm SL, temperature 24–26°C, pH 5.0–6.0. gH listed as 5–12 dH appears inconsistent with blackwater peat-swamp ecology; 0–5 used per SeriouslyFish and ecological data. pH averaged to 4.0–6.5 per SeriouslyFish as more ecologically accurate.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-check for size (0.5–0.8 in = 1.3–2.0 cm), temperature (24–26°C), pH (4.5–6.8), gH (1–5 dGH), lifespan (3–5 years), tankmates, and breeding notes. 5-gallon minimum tank cited; 40L used as more appropriate for a school of 8 per SeriouslyFish 45×30 cm base recommendation.

fish.breedingNotes: The Dwarf Rasbora is a continuous egg-scatterer that provides no parental care; adults will readily consume their own eggs and fry if not removed. A separate, small breeding tank furnished with fine-leaved plants such as Java moss or a spawning mop is recommended. Very soft, acidic water — around pH 5.8–6.3 and 1–3 dGH — is generally advised for successful spawning. Fry are extremely small at hatching and require microscopic food such as infusoria or paramecium before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii.
